lessons.body: print converts values to text, writes them to stdout, and adds a newline unless told otherwise.
lessons.example:
name = 'Ada'
score = 7
print('example:', f'{name}:{score}')
lessons.starter:
name = 'Ada'
score = 7
# TODO: format the variables for stdout
print('TODO')
lessons.title: print and stdout

lessons.body: Assignment binds a name to an object; rebinding changes what the name points at, not the old object.
lessons.example:
count = 1
count = count + 2
print('example:', count)
lessons.starter:
word = 'todo'
# TODO: rebind word before it reaches stdout
print(word)
lessons.title: Variables

lessons.body: int and float cover most numeric work; //, %, and ** are common in problem solutions.
lessons.example:
total = 7
size = 2
print('example:', f'{total // size}:{total % size}')
lessons.starter:
total = 7
size = 2
# TODO: use integer division and remainder together
print(f'{total / size}:0')
lessons.title: Numbers

lessons.body: Strings are immutable sequences, so indexing and slicing read characters without changing the original text.
lessons.example:
text = 'python'
print('example:', text[1:4])
lessons.starter:
text = 'xokx'
# TODO: slice away the marker characters before printing
print(text)
lessons.title: Strings

lessons.body: if chooses a block, for iterates over an iterable, and while repeats until its condition changes.
lessons.starter:
total = 0
for n in range(1, 4):
    # TODO: add only odd numbers
    total += 0
print(total)
lessons.title: Control flow

lessons.body: def creates a callable object; parameters receive arguments and return sends a value back to the caller.
lessons.starter:
def area(width, height):
    # TODO: return rectangle area, not perimeter
    return width + height

print(area(3, 4))
lessons.title: Functions

lessons.body: stdin starts as text; read it once, split into tokens when structure matters, then convert tokens explicitly.
lessons.example:
import sys
nums = [int(token) for token in sys.stdin.read().split()]
print('example:', sum(nums))
lessons.starter:
import sys
nums = []
# TODO: parse all integers from stdin and print their sum
print(sum(nums))
lessons.title: Input parsing

lessons.body: Lists keep ordered values by position; dicts map keys to values for direct lookup and counting.
lessons.example:
scores = {'Ada': [2, 3], 'Lin': [4]}
print('example:', sum(scores['Ada']))
lessons.starter:
nums = [2, 3]
scores = {'Ada': nums}
# TODO: print the sum stored under Ada without hard-coding 5
print(len(scores['Ada']))
lessons.title: Lists and dicts

lessons.body: Tuples group a fixed sequence of values; sets keep unique members and make membership checks cheap.
lessons.example:
pair = ('o', 'k')
seen = set(pair)
print('example:', ''.join(pair), len(seen))
lessons.starter:
pair = ('o', 'k')
seen = set()
# TODO: build a set from the tuple and print ok 2
print(''.join(pair[:1]), len(seen))
lessons.title: Tuples and sets

lessons.body: A comprehension combines an output expression, a loop, and optional filters into one collection-building expression.
lessons.example:
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4]
squares = [n * n for n in nums if n % 2 == 0]
print('example:', sum(squares))
lessons.starter:
letters = ['o', 'x', 'k']
# TODO: keep only the letters needed for ok with a comprehension
word = ''.join([ch for ch in letters if ch != 'x' and ch != 'k'])
print(word)
lessons.title: Comprehensions

lessons.body: try isolates code that may fail; except handles a specific recoverable error without hiding unrelated bugs.
lessons.title: Exceptions

lessons.body: with enters a managed scope and calls cleanup automatically; file handles and contextlib helpers use this pattern.
lessons.example:
from io import StringIO

with StringIO('ok') as handle:
    text = handle.read()
print('example:', text)
lessons.starter:
from io import StringIO

with StringIO('ok') as handle:
    # TODO: read from the managed handle before it closes
    text = ''
print(text)
lessons.title: Files and context managers

lessons.body: import binds a module or object name so code can reuse standard-library behavior instead of rewriting it.
lessons.example:
import math

print('example:', math.ceil(2.1))
lessons.starter:
import math

# TODO: use the imported module to round upward
print(math.floor(2.1))
lessons.title: Modules and imports

lessons.body: dataclass generates the routine class methods for simple data containers while leaving behavior explicit.
lessons.example:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Point:
    x: int
    y: int

point = Point(2, 3)
print('example:', point.x + point.y)
lessons.starter:
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class Point:
    x: int
    y: int

point = Point(2, 3)
# TODO: use both fields
print(point.x)
lessons.title: Dataclasses

lessons.body: Type hints document expected shapes for readers and tools; Python still executes values dynamically at runtime.
lessons.starter:
from typing import Iterable

def total(values: Iterable[int]) -> int:
    # TODO: return the sum of the iterable
    return 0

print(total([2, 3]))
lessons.title: Type hints

lessons.body: Iterators produce values one at a time; a generator function uses yield to pause and resume that production.
lessons.starter:
def words():
    # TODO: yield ok as the first generated value
    yield ''

print(next(words()))
lessons.title: Iterators and generators

lessons.body: lambda makes a small expression function; a closure remembers names from the surrounding scope.
lessons.example:
def make_adder(delta):
    return lambda value: value + delta

add_two = make_adder(2)
print('example:', add_two(3))
lessons.starter:
def make_suffix(suffix):
    # TODO: return a lambda that appends suffix to word
    return lambda word: word

add_ok = make_suffix('ok')
print(add_ok(''))
lessons.title: Lambdas and closures

lessons.body: A decorator receives a function at definition time and returns the function object that name should now refer to.
lessons.title: Decorators

lessons.body: sorted returns a new ordered list; key functions choose the value used for each comparison.
lessons.example:
users = [('Ada', 3), ('Lin', 5), ('Bo', 4)]
best = sorted(users, key=lambda item: item[1], reverse=True)[0]
print('example:', f'{best[0]}:{best[1]}')
lessons.starter:
users = [('Ada', 3), ('Lin', 5), ('Bo', 4)]
# TODO: sort by score descending, not by name
best = sorted(users)[0]
print(f'{best[0]}:{best[1]}')
lessons.title: Sorting and key functions

lessons.body: Counter counts hashable values directly; defaultdict creates missing collection values when grouping.
lessons.example:
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

words = ['red', 'blue', 'red']
counts = Counter(words)
groups = defaultdict(list)
for word in words:
    groups[word[0]].append(word)
print('example:', counts['red'], len(groups['r']))
lessons.starter:
from collections import Counter, defaultdict

words = ['red', 'blue', 'red']
counts = Counter()
groups = defaultdict(list)
# TODO: count words and group them by first letter
print(counts['red'], len(groups['r']))
lessons.title: Counter and defaultdict

lessons.body: deque supports efficient appends and pops on both ends, which is why it is the usual queue type.
lessons.example:
from collections import deque

queue = deque(['middle'])
queue.appendleft('start')
queue.append('end')
print('example:', queue.popleft(), queue.pop())
lessons.starter:
from collections import deque

queue = deque(['middle'])
# TODO: add start on the left and end on the right
print(queue.popleft(), 'missing')
lessons.title: deque

lessons.body: itertools provides lazy iterator building blocks for pairing, chaining, slicing, and combinatorics.
lessons.example:
import itertools

parts = [['o'], ['k']]
print('example:', ''.join(itertools.chain.from_iterable(parts)))
lessons.starter:
import itertools

parts = [['o'], ['k']]
# TODO: flatten both inner lists lazily
print(''.join(itertools.chain.from_iterable(parts[:1])))
lessons.title: itertools

lessons.body: pathlib represents paths as objects, so code can ask for names, suffixes, and parents without manual string splitting.
lessons.example:
from pathlib import PurePosixPath

path = PurePosixPath('logs/app.txt')
print('example:', f'{path.stem}:{path.suffix}')
lessons.starter:
from pathlib import PurePosixPath

path = PurePosixPath('logs/app.txt')
# TODO: combine the stem and suffix properties
print(path.name)
lessons.title: pathlib

lessons.body: assert checks an invariant in small examples; test frameworks build on the same idea with repeatable test functions.
lessons.example:
def add_two(value):
    return value + 2

assert add_two(3) == 5
print('example:', 'ok')
lessons.starter:
def add_two(value):
    # TODO: make the assertion describe the intended behavior
    return value

assert add_two(3) == 3
print('todo')
lessons.title: Testing and assert

lessons.body: async def creates a coroutine; await pauses until the awaited operation completes, and asyncio.run drives the top-level coroutine.
lessons.example:
import asyncio

async def label():
    return 'ok'

async def main():
    print('example:', await label())

asyncio.run(main())
lessons.starter:
import asyncio

async def label():
    return 'ok'

async def main():
    # TODO: await the coroutine before printing
    result = 'pending'
    print(result)

asyncio.run(main())
lessons.title: Async concepts
